perturbation.original_text: Recipient understands and agrees that Company’s client and prospective client lists, proposals, reports, all internal memoranda and documents and any information communicated to Recipient, written or oral, that is indicated as confidential, are trade secrets within the meaning of Chapter 19.108 of the Revised Code of Washington, and that misappropriation of such information, as defined in said Chapter, shall subject Recipient to penalties as set forth in said Chapter.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient understands and agrees that Company’s client and prospective client lists, proposals, reports, all internal memoranda and documents and any information communicated to Recipient, written or oral, that is indicated as confidential, are confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the reference to the Revised Code of Washington (RCW) Chapter 19.108 and the statement about misappropriation penalties, the contract weakens the legal protection of the client list as a trade secret under Washington law. This weakens legal recourse for the Company in case of violations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: RCW 19.108 - Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the explicit acknowledgment of client lists, proposals, reports and internal documents as trade secrets under RCW 19.108, effectively weakening the protection afforded by the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act and potentially limiting the company's ability to pursue legal action for misappropriation under the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Chapter 19.108 RCW: UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 Complete Chapter HTML PDF | RCW Dispositions Chapter 19.108 RCW UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Sections HTML PDF 19.108.010 Definitions. HTML PDF 19.108.020 Remedies for misappropriation — Injunction, royalty. HTML PDF 19.108.030 Remedies for misappropriation — Damages. HTML PDF 19.108.040 Award of attorney's fees. HTML PDF 19.108.050 Court orders to preserve secrecy of alleged trade secrets. HTML PDF 19.108.060 Actions for misappropriation — Time limitation. HTML PDF 19.108.900 Effect of chapter on other law. NOTES: Requiring assignment of employee's rights to inventions: RCW 49.44.140 , 49.44.150 .
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly references Chapter 19.108 of the Revised Code of Washington, which is the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. By removing this reference and the statement about misappropriation penalties, the changed text weakens the link to the specific legal protections afforded under Washington law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows that Chapter 19.108 RCW defines remedies for misappropriation. The omission reduces the company's ability to pursue legal action for trade secret violations under this statute, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to use all reasonable precautions, no less than Recipient’s treatment of its own Confidential Information of a similar nature, to prevent the unauthorized disclosure of the Company’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees to use reasonable precautions to prevent the unauthorized disclosure of the Company’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'no less than Recipient's treatment of its own Confidential Information of a similar nature,' the contract weakens the standard of care required from the Recipient. Washington law requires a certain level of due diligence to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: RCW 19.108.010(4) - Definition of Misappropriation requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text omits the standard of care that the Recipient must use at least the same level of precautions as they do with their own Confidential Information. RCW 19.108.010(4) defines misappropriation as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent by one who used improper means to acquire knowledge of it, or who at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it. The company now has a weaker defense if trade secrets are not properly protected, or there are less protections than what the Recipient uses.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.010: Definitions. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.010 Beginning of Chapter << 19.108.010 >> 19.108.020 PDF RCW 19.108.010 Definitions. Unless the context clearly requires otherwise, the definitions set forth in this section apply throughout this chapter. (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes a specific standard of care, namely that the Recipient must use precautions 'no less than Recipient’s treatment of its own Confidential Information of a similar nature'. This weakens the protection of trade secrets. The Law Explanation states that this weaker protection provides the company with 'a weaker defense if trade secrets are not properly protected.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, RCW 19.108.010, defines 'misappropriation' which involves the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent. The omission of the standard of care directly impacts the ability to prevent misappropriation, creating a legal contradiction, and making the contract potentially non-compliant with trade secret protections.

perturbation.original_text: Upon demand by the Company or termination of this Agreement, all materials bearing or disclosing any of the Confidential Information shall be delivered or returned to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, all materials bearing or disclosing any of the Confidential Information shall be delivered or returned to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "Upon demand by the Company", the agreement no longer allows the company to demand the return of confidential information during the term of the agreement, even if there is a legitimate reason to do so. This could hinder the company's ability to protect its trade secrets if the recipient is mishandling the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 19.108.020 states that injunctive relief is available to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. By removing the company's right to demand the return of confidential information, the company's ability to obtain injunctive relief in a timely manner is hindered, as the company must now wait until the termination of the agreement to recover its materials.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.020: Remedies for misappropriation—Injunction, royalty. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.020 19.108.010 << 19.108.020 >> 19.108.030 PDF RCW 19.108.020 Remedies for misappropriation — Injunction, royalty. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. [ 1981 c 286 s 2 .]
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows the company to demand confidential information, while the modified version removes this right, only allowing for the return of materials upon termination. The Law Explanation states that RCW 19.108.020 allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing RCW 19.108.020, states, "Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." By removing the company's ability to demand the return of information, the company's ability to prevent misappropriation is hindered, as they must now wait until termination, weakening their ability to obtain timely injunctive relief as per the law.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient may disclose the Company’s Confidential Information if and only to the extent that a disclosure is required by applicable law, provided that Recipient uses reasonable efforts to limit the disclosure by means of a protective order or a request for Confidential/Attorneys’ Eyes Only treatment that permits the Company a reasonable opportunity to review the proposed disclosure before it is made, and to interpose its own objection to the disclosure and, if appropriate, bring a motion for a protective order.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may disclose the Company’s Confidential Information if and only to the extent that a disclosure is required by applicable law.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for the Recipient to seek a protective order or allow the Company to review the disclosure before it is made eliminates the safeguard preventing a wider dissemination of confidential information than absolutely necessary under the law. This weakens the company's trade secret protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: RCW 19.108.020 - Injunctive Relief, specifically regarding efforts to preserve secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The removal of the clause requiring 'reasonable efforts to limit the disclosure by means of a protective order or a request for Confidential/Attorneys’ Eyes Only treatment' contradicts the intent of RCW 19.108.020, which allows for injunctive relief to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. By not requiring these protections, the agreement fails to ensure that reasonable measures are taken to maintain the secrecy of the confidential information, which in turn weakens the company's ability to seek injunctive relief should a wider disclosure occur.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.020: Remedies for misappropriation—Injunction, royalty. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.020 19.108.010 << 19.108.020 >> 19.108.030 PDF RCW 19.108.020 Remedies for misappropriation — Injunction, royalty. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. [ 1981 c 286 s 2 .]
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for the Recipient to seek a protective order or allow the Company to review the disclosure before it is made, which weakens trade secret protection. RCW 19.108.020 allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. By removing the requirement to seek a protective order, the agreement fails to take reasonable measures to maintain the confidentiality of the information, which in turn weakens the company's ability to seek injunctive relief should a wider disclosure occur. The change contradicts the intent of the law by not ensuring reasonable measures are taken to maintain secrecy.
